question.text: Mrs. Desai, aged 70, has been receiving reverse mortgage payments of Rs. 35,000 per month for the past 10 years. She wants to know her income tax liability on these receipts. What is the correct position?

answer.text: The receipts are taxable as income from house property
The receipts are taxable as income from other sources after Rs. 50,000 exemption
Only the interest component embedded in receipts is taxable
All receipts from reverse mortgage are fully exempt from income tax under Section 10(43)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** All amounts received under a reverse mortgage scheme are fully exempt from income tax under Section 10(43) of the Income Tax Act. This applies regardless of the amount received or the tenure of receipt. The borrower does not need to include these receipts in taxable income. **The Trap:** Candidates may think the receipts are classified under some income head like "house property" or "other sources." The specific Section 10(43) exemption is designed to ensure senior citizens receive the full benefit without tax erosion. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers tax exemptions for retirement products. Section 10(43) is specifically relevant to reverse mortgage. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The tax-free nature of reverse mortgage receipts is a crucial advantage for senior citizens in higher tax brackets. Combined with no prepayment penalty, it provides flexible and tax-efficient retirement income.
